e-P5IFTqB98.1: And what happens if you fall into one?
e-P5IFTqB98.2: Stars are incredibly massive collections of mostly hydrogen atoms
e-P5IFTqB98.3: that collapse from enormous gas clouds under their own gravity.
e-P5IFTqB98.4: In their core, nuclear fusion crushes hydrogen atoms into helium,
e-P5IFTqB98.5: releasing a tremendous amount of energy.
e-P5IFTqB98.6: This energy, in the form of radiation, pushes against gravity,
e-P5IFTqB98.7: maintaining a delicate balance between the two forces.
e-P5IFTqB98.8: As long as there is fusion in the core, a star remains stable enough.
e-P5IFTqB98.9: But for stars with way more mass than our own sun,

e5BFR-E-ae0.0: Pangaea. Over millions of years, Pangaea split apart, causing the dinosaurs to become separated
e5BFR-E-ae0.1: for the very first time. This led the reptiles to adapt to their specific habitats and diversify,
e5BFR-E-ae0.2: giving rise to many new dinosaur species. According to some estimates, more than 1,000
e5BFR-E-ae0.3: species of dinosaurs have roamed the Earth. All dinosaurs descended from reptiles called
e5BFR-E-ae0.4: archosaurs. From there, dinosaurs branched out into two major groups, Saurischia and
e5BFR-E-ae0.5: Ornithischia, based on the shape and orientation of their pelvis. In saurischian dinosaurs,
e5BFR-E-ae0.6: such as Tyrannosaurus rex and Brachiosaurus, the pelvis's pubis bone faces forward and
e5BFR-E-ae0.7: down. In ornithischian dinosaurs, such as Stegosaurus and Hadrosaurus, the pubis bone
e5BFR-E-ae0.8: faces backward and down. Across both groups, there are many different types of dinosaurs.
